1: Ahab had seventy sons in Samaria — the number signals the scale of royal polygamy and the extent of the dynasty's administrative reach; 70 is also the number of Jacob's descendants in Egypt (Exodus 1:5) and the 70 elders who accompanied Moses (Exodus 24:1). Jehu writes to the 'rulers of Jezreel' (reading with LXX), the city elders, and the guardians of the royal sons, proposing they choose the best man to fight him or submit.
2: The letter frames a stark choice: if they have chariots, horses, a fortified city, and weapons, they should choose the best of their master's sons, set him on his father's throne, and fight for the house of Ahab. The offer is not sincere — Jehu knows they will capitulate; the letter is a legal record showing that Samaria was given the choice to resist.
3: The elders and guardians respond immediately with fear — 'two kings could not stand before him; how can we stand?' The rapid capitulation echoes Rahab's assessment of Israel's crossing (Joshua 2:10-11); military reputation as a submission catalyst. They submit without a fight.
4: Jehu's second letter is more explicit: if you are on my side, bring me the heads of the men of your master's sons tomorrow at this time in Jezreel. The demand for heads was an ancient Near Eastern proof-of-submission — delivering the heads of the deposed dynasty's children signals irreversible commitment to the new ruler.
5: The seventy sons of Ahab are killed by the guardians; their heads are put in baskets and sent to Jehu in Jezreel. The scale of the execution — seventy deaths in one night, organized by the very men charged to protect them — shows the completeness of the Omride dynasty's political collapse.
6: The heads arrive in two heaps at the gate — a public display at the city gate was the standard ancient Near Eastern form of military announcement and warning. Jehu emerges in the morning and addresses the assembled people: 'You are righteous (ṣaddîqîm ʾattem) — it is I who conspired against my master and killed him; but who struck all these?'
7: Jehu's rhetorical move is subtle: he absolves himself of personal conspiracy by pointing to the Samarian elders' action, while also noting that this fulfills YHWH's oracle. He frames the corporate execution as divine confirmation — YHWH has done what he spoke through Elijah; nothing can fall to the ground of YHWH's word.
8: Jehu strikes all who remained of the house of Ahab in Jezreel — every great man, every acquaintance, every priest. The purge extends to the social network of the Omride court, not just blood relatives. Priests (likely Baal priests associated with the court) are included in the sweep.
9: On the way from Jezreel to Samaria, at Beth-eked of the Shepherds, Jehu encounters the brothers of Ahaziah of Judah — forty-two kinsmen of the Judahite king. They are going down to visit the sons of the king and the queen mother (not yet knowing of the deaths). Jehu has them taken and killed at the pit of Beth-eked — forty-two men, none escaped.
10: Jonadab son of Rechab comes to meet Jehu. The Rechabites were a clan that maintained a nomadic, non-agricultural lifestyle as a religious commitment — no wine, no houses, no vineyards (Jeremiah 35:6-7). Their founder Jonadab's alliance with Jehu's anti-Baal purge suggests a shared anti-Canaanite-syncretism stance; they rejected the agricultural cult that Baal worship represented.
11: Jehu's question to Jonadab: 'Is your heart right, as my heart is right with your heart?' (hăyēšār libbəḵā ʿim-libbî kaʾăšer libbî ʿim-libbəḵā) — a covenant-loyalty formula; 'right heart' (lēḇāḇ yāšār) is not merely personal sincerity but covenant alignment. When Jonadab affirms, Jehu says 'Give me your hand' — the handshake as covenant seal.
12: Jonadab rides in Jehu's chariot — a public display of alliance. The Rechabite presence at Jehu's side during the Baal-temple purge legitimizes the action in the eyes of conservative Yahwists; Jonadab's reputation for purity validates Jehu's religious agenda.
13: When Jehu comes to Samaria he kills all who remained of Ahab in Samaria until he had annihilated them, 'according to the word of YHWH which he spoke to Elijah' — the third explicit prophetic-fulfillment notice in these two chapters (vv9:27, 2 Kings 9:36, 2 Kings 10:17). The repetition is the historian's theological structure: Jehu's coup is interpretively framed as prophetic completion throughout.
14: Jehu's anti-Baal operation: he gathers all the people and announces that Ahab served Baal a little but Jehu will serve Baal much — a deceptive announcement, a ruse de guerre to gather Baal worshippers. He summons all Baal's prophets, servants, and priests — none are to be absent on pain of death.
15: The Baal worshippers arrive; the house of Baal is filled from end to end. Jehu then orders vestments for all Baal's worshippers — the distribution of official cult garments serves the tactical purpose of identifying all present as Baal devotees and preventing any infiltrators from escaping the purge.
16: Jehu station eighty men outside with the order: 'The man who allows any of the men whom I bring into your hands to escape shall forfeit his life.' The perimeter is sealed before the massacre begins — a military encirclement operation applied to a cultic building.
17: When the burnt offering was finished, Jehu commanded: 'Go in, strike them down; let not a man come out.' The burnt offering was used as a cover — the assembly format of a religious ceremony prevented early escape. The guards strike them down; the executioners throw the bodies out and go into the inner shrine of the house of Baal.
18: They bring out the pillar (maṣṣēḇôṯ) of Baal's house and burn it. They pull down the maṣṣēḇāh of Baal and tear down the house of Baal and make it a latrine (məḥărāʾôṯ) — the latrine desecration is the definitive anti-cultic act: the most sacred space of Baalism is converted to the most polluting use. The pattern matches Josiah's later desecrations (2 Kings 23:8, 10).
19: Baal is destroyed from Israel — the text is explicit: wayyašmēḏ ʾeṯ-habbáʿal miyyiśrāʾēl. But (v29) Jehu does not depart from the sins of Jeroboam, which made Israel to sin — the golden calves at Bethel and Dan are left standing. The anti-Baal purge is complete; the anti-Jeroboam reform is not attempted. Jehu destroys the foreign cult but not the domestic idolatry.
20: YHWH commends Jehu: 'Because you have done well in carrying out what is right in my eyes and have done to the house of Ahab all that was in my heart, your sons shall sit on the throne of Israel to the fourth generation.' The conditional promise gives Jehu's dynasty the longest tenure in northern history — four generations (Jehoahaz, Jehoash, Jeroboam II, Zechariah) — explicitly limited to four as a reward-not-a-covenant structure.
21: But Jehu was not careful to walk in the law of YHWH the God of Israel with all his heart — lōʾ šāmar lāleḵeṯ bəṯôraṯ YHWH ʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾēl bəkol-libbô. The qualification mirrors the standard regnal evaluation; the four-generation reward is given despite incomplete fidelity. YHWH's sovereignty does not require its human instruments to be fully righteous to accomplish his purposes.
22: In Jehu's time Hazael began to cut off parts of Israel; the Aramean pressure that characterized Jehoahaz's reign begins to build. Jehu's reign is marked by territorial loss to Aram even as his purge secured internal religious control — a trade-off that shows the limits of a purely coup-based reform.
23: Jehu dies and is buried in Samaria; Jehoahaz his son reigns in his place. The death notice is brief; the four-generation promise begins its count here. The Jehu dynasty is the only northern dynasty that extends beyond two generations.
24: The twenty-eight year reign of Jehu is the longest single reign of any northern king except Jeroboam II (forty-one years). The length of the dynasty, combined with Jeroboam II's military successes, makes the Jehu dynasty the high-water mark of the northern kingdom — which makes its end (Zechariah's assassination, 2 Kings 15:10) all the more sudden.
25: Chronological note linking Jehu's accession to Joram's twelfth year in Judah — the synchronism anchors Jehu's dynasty in the bi-national chronological framework that runs throughout Kings. The precision of Kings' chronology (despite its complexity) reflects an archival tradition of royal synchronism.
26: YHWH's assessment of Jehu — the act of destroying the Baal cult was 'what was right in YHWH's eyes' — but the persistent Jeroboam-golden-calf worship meant that Israel continued in its structural sin. Divine evaluation distinguishes between what was done right and what remained incomplete; the commission to destroy Baal was fulfilled; the broader covenant restoration was not.
27: The attack on Israel's territory by Hazael — cutting off parts of Gilead from the Arnon to Gilead — points to the military cost of the internal purge; Jehu's energies were consumed by regime change rather than border defense. Israel lost the Transjordanian territories to Aram during Jehu's reign, territories that Jeroboam II would later recover.
28: The archival notice about Jehu's acts in the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Israel closes the chapter; the comprehensive purge narrative (2 Kings 9-10) stands as the most detailed single military-political operation in Kings, a literary monument to the fulfillment of the Elijah-Jezebel-Ahab oracle sequence.
29: The remaining summary: Jehu's son Jehoahaz reigns in his place; the four-generation promise begins its fulfillment. The dynasty that started with a coup against Joram (Ahab's son) will itself be terminated by a coup against Zechariah (Jehu's great-great-grandson) in 2 Kings 15:10 — the same mechanism of violent regime change that began the dynasty ends it.
30: Final synchronism: Jehu reigned twenty-eight years over Israel in Samaria. The number closes the chapter on a reign that was simultaneously the most theologically significant (Baal-destruction, prophetic fulfillment) and politically consequential (launching the most durable northern dynasty) of any northern king.
31: The territorial summary: all the territory that Hazael took from Israel east of the Jordan — from Aroer by the Arnon Valley through Gilead and Bashan — was lost to Aram during Jehu's dynasty. The military weakness that accompanied the Baal purge created the opening for Aramean expansion that would not be reversed until Jeroboam II.
32: The note about YHWH's anger beginning to reduce Israel signals the theological framework: the Jehu purge satisfied the Ahab-Jezebel covenant debt but did not restore full covenant obedience; the trajectory of divine judgment continues because the Jeroboam golden calves remain. Reform without full covenant renewal leaves the structural problem intact.
33: Summary of the Hazael losses: all the land of Gilead, from Aroer to the Arnon (the southern Transjordanian boundary) through Gilead and Bashan. These were the territories of Gad and Reuben and Manasseh. The loss of the Transjordan strips Israel to the western bank, reducing the kingdom to its minimal core.
34: The archival formula for Jehu closes chapter 10: the rest of his acts, all he did, all his might — in the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Israel. The 'might' reference (gəḇûrāṯô) is used for Jehu despite the territorial losses; military and political decisiveness apparently qualified for the 'might' designation regardless of strategic outcome.
35: Jehu sleeps with his fathers and they bury him in Samaria. The burial in Samaria is the standard northern-kingdom burial location — none of the northern kings are buried in Jerusalem. The dynastic continuity (Jehoahaz succeeds) begins the four-generation countdown of YHWH's promise in v30.
36: The final verse confirms twenty-eight years for Jehu's reign over Israel in Samaria — the archival number matching the synchronistic framework of the chronology. The reign that began with a coup at Ramoth-gilead ends with a burial in Samaria, the capital the Omrides built and Jehu inherited.
